nd it im| Darleo keep §any sort of’ real

interest in the political horse-play of the Unionists.
They are poor in ability and bankrupt in policy. Here
is a brilliant ‘“Irish’’ suggestion from the Irish
" Times:—
So far as our observation goes, the one fiscal
. proposal on which all Tariff Reformers are agreed
is a tax on manufactured goods. If Mr. Bonar
Law were to make a speech definitely stating that
& tax of five or ten per cent. on manufactured
goods would be the limit of the tariff changes pro-
posed during the first Unionist Parliament, and
_that no new taxes upon food must be proposed
until there had been another appeal to the people,
we believe that he would bring great relief to a large
number of Unionist candidates, who at present
have to talk at large upon the subject because they

have no definite scheme to go upon, and would
materially improve the prospects of his party.

There’s brains for you! Everyone knows that Ire-
land is behind—and artificially behind, if we may put it
that way—on her manufacturing side. England and
Scotland do her manufactures for her; the amount of
non-British-manufactured goods imported to Ireland
is not proportionately very large, so far as we know.
The struggle of Irish manufacture, actual and poten-
tial, is with British manufactures in the main. Take a
few items at random—tobacco, for instance, is up
against the British Combine; soap is against the St.
Helen’s combination ; cloth is against the British manu-
facturers ; matches, we believe, are mainly against the
English manufacturers; we know of no competitors,
except British, up against Irish-made candles; boots
are against Northampton and other English boot
centres; cutlery is against Sheffield ; bicycles are against
Coventry, and so on. If we believed in the principle
of legislative tarifis—as a matter of fact, we are dead
against them as an economic policy—we would, of
course, be mainly concerned to have tariffs against
English manufacture. But here we have 'a paper
calling itself the Irish Times—a paper that has not the
common decency to utter an adequate protest against
the hellish bigotry and savagery that is being dealt out
to unfortunate Catholic workmen who are terrorised
and assaulted out of earning their living in ship yards
in Belfast—suggesting that British and Irish manu-
factured articles should be protected from the rest of
the world by a tariff of five or ten per cent.! Irish con-
sumers of manufactured goods would have the privilege
of paying artificially inflated prices for many of the
manufactured articles they used, whilst as between
Trish manufactures and their British competitors—our
boot, cloth, match, soap and candle makers—the Irish
manufacturers would have no advantage whatever in
relation to their British competitors. ~ And this bril-
liant suggestion is trotted out by the Irish Times. What
do Irish manufacturers who are Unionists think of the
silly proposal, a proposal that would add yet another
handicap to Ireland and give her no advantage? This
is more Irish Unionist ‘‘ statesmanship ** |

Navan is the capital of bullock county, one of the
most wasteful and in some respects most useless
counties in Ireland. Navan once had a bacon factory,
but it did not last long, and we believe it still has a
furniture factory though we never hear tidings of it-
Navan is just the flabby useless place that would flop
before a bobby. The bobby in Ireland is the uni-
ormed symbol of laziness and wastefulness, and it is
fitting, perhaps, that the capital of bullock land
should bow down before him. Policeman Delahunty
is retiring, if he has not.already retired, from Navan.
What did he ever do for his pay but crease his hair and
polish his boots, which are the principal occupations
of policemen in this country? Navan is, we under-
stand, o great town for betting; a town that could not
run a bacon factory is just the place where the
‘“ bookie ’’ would thrive. There are a couple of Bungs

“on the committee organised to promote the testimonial

for the grest man who is leavig Navan. The com-
mittee are:—E. O'Reilly, a draper and ex-U.D.C.;
Mat Meleady, a Bung; J. Gibney, J. A, Jordan, J.
Kearney, Gus Hanley, N. Delany, T. Noonan, a soli-
citor; J. Russell, stationmaster; J. Coldrick, a but-
chei ; E. Gilsenan, J. Doran, & Bung; and J. Keap-
pock.

We enjoy having. occasioi—and' we have many
sccasions—of writing' at length or ‘commenting on a
successful Irigh industry. It i§ with different feelings
that we wiite on the decline, or lack of success, of
any Irish industry. We regret to hear that Messrs.
A, Yeomans and Co., Ltd., of 57 William Street,
Dublin, have resolved to go into voluntary, liquidation.
Of late years Messrs. Yeomans carried on a big industry
in the making of ladies’ hats; and at their works, which
included furs and millinery, they employed at times
up to 200 hands. Mr. Yeomans started business in
Dublin about 17 years ago, in Beresford Place, and the
hat depdrtment of the industry has been in existence
for about eight years. The present’ company of A.
Yeomans and Co.; Ltd., was floated about five years
ago, but from the start it was—like some other Irish
ventures—handicapped for want of adequate capital.
If there had been sufficient capital we are informed that
the business would have thrived exceedingly, and even
ab present a few energetic men with adequate capital—
and thé capital that would be adequate is compara-
tivély small-—could, we are assured, achieve certain
success. The fact that the business has lasted so long
indicates that there is potential success in it. Messrs.
Yeomans have trained a large amount of labour in their
time—some of the girls have worked for the firm for
16 or 17 years, and it is unpleasant to contemplate
shis labour being displaced. =~ We wonder how much
Irish money was lost in rubber and in other specula-
tions? There is, however, a great scarcity of money
when an industry needs a little capital. One would
think that some of the big drapery houses would come
to the support of an industry like this; and even if it
is'sold up and realised, one of the big houses might do
worse than run it, as the machinery and the trained
labour is to be had.

It is not so long ago since all Ireland outside the
Eollege Green Spelling Bee circles was contemptuously
laughing at the Trinity Medical School. ~ On Friday
the Freeman devoted its first leading article to
“ Trinity College Medical- School,” and the first
zentence is as follows:—*‘ All Irishmen will unite in
congratulations to Trinity College on the Bi-centenary
of its Medical School.”” This is inaccurate, so far as
we are concerned.  Why should Irishmen congratu-
[ate Queen Bess’s English establishment in this coun-
try on anything the old anti-Irish sinner did?  This
University is represented in the British Parliament by
@Garson and Jim Crow, K.C., and recently Dublin had
an experience of the manners of Trinity Cads when
they went rioting through the streets of Dublin. About
all we know about this Trinity hub-hub is that during
one or two days last week at every corner within a big
radius of College Green you were liable to bob up
against a lot of old chaps, many of them sporting
beards and dressed up in scarlet and other colours as
if they had been taking part in one of those objection-
able and imported musical comedies that TLondon
dumps into Dublin. Trinity has made Mr. Sherlock
a wise LI.D. ‘‘Dr.’’ Sherlock gave the Mansion House
for' the inevitable banquet, at which he, by thé way,
was represented by his locum, Mr. Beattie. Of course,
the first: toast at the banquet was *‘ The King.”

" @n Saturday. the  Trish Times ' came oub with a
characteristic article on. the Trinity business with a
little plamas for ““ Dr.”” Sherlock in the first part and

Referring to foreign visitors, the
marks —

They heard only the other day from a great
ccclesi)nst'ical autl{oriby ‘that the old Roman
Catholic boycott of Trinity College still obtains.
They find themselves welcomed by the Nutlonphsﬁ
Lord Mayor of Dublin, whom to-day the Univer-
sity will honour with its Degree. 5
lock’s courtesy the graduates’ banqueb was he
in the historic Round Room of the Mansion House
—an incident which we regard as wholly gratify-
ing and satisfactory.

Ts not this like suggesting that there was & deal—
that the Mansion House was secured for the evening,.
not for £ s. d. but for I.1.D.? The ** Irish Times
even goes out of its way to pay a compliment to the
“ Freeman "'—a fact which strengthens our view that
the * Freeman '’ was wealk-kneed in this matter. The
“Trish Times " says that ** They (the Lord Mayor and
Corporation of Dublin) are rightly proud of this greab
national institution.” Queen Be English fortress
in Ireland is a ** great national institution,” of which
“Dr.”” Sherlock, the latest Trinity - Cawtholic, is
proud! The ‘‘ Trish Times "’ having laid soft plaster
on the  Freeman '’ and on our latest LI.D. for their
little efforts to bolster up Trinity, goes on to attack
certain. Hibernians and make excuses for the anti-
Catholic savages of Belfast. It says, ‘ The attack by
a body of Hibernians on a party of children at Castle-
dawson was a truly abominable business.”” But Mr.
Devlin in Parliament on Friday said of that matter,
““We deny your allegations. We challenge a com-
plete enquiry, and we ask the Chief Secretary to give
it to us. There was no assaults on women or on chil-
dren. A crowd of Hibernians resented an attack made
upon them by a band of Orangemen.’”’ Of course that
is ignored by the ‘ Irish Times,” because it had to
refer at last to the awful brutality and hellish conduct;
of Carson’s and Campbell’s dupes in Belfast. ~ Whilst
Trinity has wheedled “‘ Dr."” Sherlock with an LL.D:,
and has thrown a compliment to the *“ Freeman,”” the
dupes and followers of the members for Trinity, Carson
and Campbell, were acting like fiends in Belfast, and
terrorising thousands of Catholic workmen from earn-
ing their bread. Carson’s work is breaking out in
Belfast, but the ‘‘ Trish Times ' dishonestly and fool-
ishly—for the dishonesty won't deceive anyone in Ire-
land—says :—

But, while these Belfast ruffians are primarily
responsible for their own conduct, a great respon-
sibility rests upon the politicians who have created
this atmosphere of violence. The present disor-
ders in Belfast—and we fear that they are not
ended—are the direct result of the Government’s
attempt to force Home Rule on Ulster. Normally,
the two sections: in Belfast live together in a sort
of guarded amity: their social relations are
friendly, if restrained. The fatal question of Home
Rule fans the slumbering passions into flame.

Of course the politicians who have helped to create this
‘“ abmosphere of violence "’ are Carson, Campbell, and
the others, including one or move Privy Councillors,
who ought to be in jail long ago for inciting to outrage
and disorder if even-handed justice had been dealt out
to them.

- A correspondent writes:—* I find o difficulty in get-
ting several articles of Irish manufacture in Skerries.
Surely it is time that the shopkeepers of this town
Woke up to the fact that there is a movement in favour
of Trish ‘manufacture.  We have recently seen that
Jacob’s have opened, or are about to open, a manu-
facturing branch in the neighbourhood of Liverpool.
. How is it that with Irish * Friendly * matches made
. in Dublin, so cheap and so good, that in one shop T
could only get English matches—not nearly as good
in my opinion? I don’t say that Irish matches are
nob to be got in Skerries, but in one of tlie few places
that can be-dignified by the name of shop in this town
Irish matches could not be obtained. And the
owner of this shop I suppose would esteem it a griev-
ance that Irish people go to Iinglish seaside resorts
* rather than to Skerries! The Great Northern Railway
is not the only handicap on Skerries. You, sir, have
no reason to champion the poor old ‘ Freeman,” but I
suppose you would be inclined to admit that it is the
best of the poor collection of Dublin dailies. I found it
difficult to get the ‘ Freeman’ in Skerries, and one
morning had to wait until T wrived in Dublin. A few
mornings ago I asked one of the boys who sell papers
on the way to the station for the ‘ Freeman,” but he
had not one to sell me : but the first paper in the parcel
under his arm was none other than one of the London
weekly ‘ penny dirties,’ ‘as you call them. There’s
Skerries for you! In the public thoroughfare of a
morning a newsboy could not sell me a ‘ Freeman '
but he had an English ‘ penny dirty’ exposed for sale.
Has the campaign against divty papers not evoked any
echo in Skerries yet ?  So far as I know it is impossible
to get such a thing as Irish cider in Skerries—L admit
that I see no mention of Irish-made cider in your
columns, so the makers may not wish to create any
demand for it. If I am not bothering you too much
about this pleasant and picturesque if primitive town,
with its pump water and its lack of all modern sani-
tary arrangements, I would like to ask do very many
live on tinned food? If not how explain all the
old. rusty tinware on the roads, and above all on the
sea front. On the sea front the rusty tinware is a
danger as well as a nuisance. It must be rather risky
for those who bathe on the strand and who don’t take
the precaution of wearing shoes. A heavy step down

The savagery of the bigots in Belfust: has gone se
far that the Catholics have been forced to form a Vigil-
ance Committee. Perhaps we had better quote from
the news columns of the anti-Catholic ‘*‘ Irish I'imes **
of Monday, July 8th, from a column headed ‘from
our correspondent.’’

Sunday.—A public meeting of Roman Catholie
clergymen and laymen was held in Belfast this
evening. Most Rev. Dr. Tohill, Bishop of Down
and Connor, presided. The meeting formed itself
into a Vigilance Committee, and having consi-
dered a large body of evidence as to the reign of
terror in the ‘shipyard, and the many workmen
who were seriously injured and driven out of em-
ployment, it drew ‘up the following telegram te
be sent to the Lord Lieutenant, the Chief Secre-
tary, and Mr. Joseph Devlin, M.P.:—'‘The
Catholics of Belfast in public meeting assembleé
(the Tord Bishop of the Diocese in the chair)
solemnly protest against the inaction of the autho-
rities in Belfast for the past week, who have giver
no protection to Catholic workmen or property.
We hereby call upon the Executive to send imme-
diately to Belfast an ample force of police and
military to protect the lives and persons of
Catholics from the present violent intimidation,
brutal attacks, and mob law that are being allowed
to go unchastised by the authorities.

So great has been the devilish onslaught on some
workingmen who happen to. be Catholics that the greaf:
firm of Messrs. Harland and Wolff have posted up the
following notice in windows on their_ premises :—

Unless the disturbances in these works cease
immediately, and all our hands are allowed to
follow their occupations peacefully without inter-
ference, the firm will be reluctantly compelled to
close until further notice.  Before taking ithis
regrettable step, however, the management, .being
convinced that a large majority of the -men
strongly disapprove of what is occurting, wish to
give them the opportunity of taking such: steps
amongst themselves as may render it unnecessary
to close the works.

It is terrible and significant of Orange ruffianism
to have refugees from Belfast arriving in Dublin. Does
the King’s writ run in Belfast? Has the Executive
Government abrogated its powers? :On Saturday last
the S.8. ““ Carmickfergus "’ arrived in the Liffey from
Belfast having on board about twenty fitters and
joiners who had been working in Belfast. These re-
fugees stated that they were compelled to fly from
Belfast as they were in -absolute. danger of losing their
lives. Tt appears that the religion and politics of-these
refugees  constituted the sole: reason for their being
hunted out of Belfast.

The claim of Ballyferriter, Co. Kerry, to be the capi-
tal of Irish Ireland has drawn a protest from Bally-
vourney, Co. Cork. In the course of a letter to us from
a Ballyvourney man, referring to the claim made in
our _columns a couple of weeks ago on behalf of Bally-
ferriter, he says:—'* No wonder, then, as I suppose to
have been the case, that the writer was so carried away
by enthusiasm as to claim superiority in this respect
and as it were cast a cloud over the fame Ballyvourney
has acquired as the capital of Irish Ireland. As re-
gards missions—well, it is about ten years since the
last mission was given in Ballyvourney, for not only
are the people truly Irish in theislanguage and customs,
but they are Irish even in their heart and soul and pos-
sess all the virtuous simplicity and innocence of Irish
people. The present parish priest of the parish came
] there about five years ago, totally ignorant of the Trish
| language, and still, from intercourse with the people of
the parish, he at present not only says the prayers at
Mass in Irish and reads the Gospel in Trish, but he
even hears confessions in Irish and can understand and
speak it exceadingly well.  Our present curate has
been with us but six months off an English mission, so
it would hardly be fair as yet to expect him to know
much Trish. However, it may be necessary to say that
our last curate, Fr. Hurley, left us a splendid Irish
speaker. The people of the parish themselves can all,
with very few exceptions, speak and understand Irish
tolerably well. Irish is the language mostly used by

CORRESPONDENCE.
CONCERNING PIPERS.

18 York Road, Rathmines.

Drar Mr. Eprror,—I have read with interest and
pleasure, Mr. R. J. Kelly’s excellent dissertation on

y Irish pipers, past and present, in & recent issue of

the LEADER.
Mr. Kelly, however, accidentally, T am sure, forgot

. to give credit to you for your powerful advocacy in the

cause of Irish music, vocal and instrumental, since
the foundation of your wonder-working journal.

Mr. J. S. Wayland, of Cork, deserves a niche in the
Temple of Fame for his successful efforts in the revival
of pipe playing and pipe musiec.

Captain Francis O'Neill, of Chicago, in his letter to
me dated May 9th, 1912, says:—

‘* Would you believe it, I have prepared sketches
running from a few paragraphs to a couple or three
pages of no less than 105 pipers, besides brief mention
of many more. 'Tis no boast to say this book will be
ab least as original as my last work, though of course
many of the tales of pipers, more or less
legendary, have appeared in more or less different dic-
tion in promiscuous Irish literature of over seventy
years ago. Anyway my memory and my own library
supplied nearly all my text. None of our libraries can
compare with mine in the matter of Hibernicana or
Irish musie.”’—Yours truly,

P. J. GRIFFITH.

NATIONAL HEALTH INSURANCE COMMISSION
(IRELAND).
Outdoor Staff (Health Insurance Officers).

Applications are invited for the post of Health In-
surance Officers at an annual salary of £80 rising to
£150.

The number of appointments made will be, approxi-
mately, 25 (20 for Men and 5 for Women).

It is probable that selections will be made, from suc-
cessful candidates of a small number for the post of
Supervising Officer, and these Officers will be placed
on the scale of salary £160 rising to £200.

The limits of age will be 24 to 45.

Only those candidates who, in the opinion of the In-
surance Commissioners appear to be suitable, will be
selected after they have passed a qualifying written
examination, to be held by the Civil Service Commis-
sioners, for the posts offered.

The subjects of examination will be :—Handwriting
and Spelling, Reading, Manuscript, English Composi-
tion (including ability to write a simple report) and

THE FEIS CEOIL.

OUNDED in the nineties of last century, amidst

doubts and misgivings, by the united efforts of
the Irish musicians and musical amateurs, the Feis
Ceoil held-its first festival or Feis at Dublin in May,
1897. Elvery year since then it has held a festival
nearly always in Dublin (on two occasions in Belfast)
with a slow but sure increase of usefulness in develop-
ing the musical talent of Ireland. It is surprising,
when we consider the state Ireland was musically
fifteen years ago, to see her progress; for although the
public taste is as low if not lower than ever, still the
practice of music has multiplied out of expectation.
This is all due to the Feis Ceoil, and especially is this
the case in instrumental music which, accmdmg to
reports of the judges, seems to have reached quite a
phenomenal degree of excellence, far surpassing the
choirs or other vocal sections, notwithstanding the fact
that- the Feis Ceoil has introduced some dlshnvulshed
singers who have since become celebrated all over the

culties, so mlich good work. I would not say a word
to injure them for the world. On the contrary, I.here
bear testimony to their ability and achievement. The.
Ixecutive Committee is .a model according to its’
limitations and its lights. I regret, however, to say

~ that its lights are darkened by its limitations. The
resulb is that with all the best intentions of its manage-
ment, the Feis Ceoil is not what it really ought to be—
the great national festival of music like the Eisteddfod
~ in Wales, influencing and interesting the national spirit
of our countrymen. The reason, of course, is, it is not
sufficiently Irish and depending on Ireland. That is
. the great distinction between it and the Gaelic League,
which has had such overpowering influence on Irish
life, in preparing men’s minds for all other movements,
such as liberary, industrial, agricultural, co-operative,
ate., which would all have died away but for its vitalis-
ing force.

At first, perbaps, there was some excuse for this
failing on the part cf the Feis Ceoil to probe funda-
mentals, because it was largely an organisation of
3 musicians ; and musicians, it is well known, have never
I been remarkable- for correct ideas of general culture.

To have at such a period made any Irish musician
understand that art, however it may benefit by foreign
influences, must spring from native sources, would
have been about as easy as to have made men
then believe that a person in a few years could
fly in a machine from Wales to Ireland. In-
deed to judge by the very general contempt
among our musicians for the folk music of Ire-
land, I doubt whether this fundamental idea is even
to-day understood by them to any extent. Moreover,
there were then as now among us several capable musi-
clans of such complete English traditions and habits
that they would have looked with horror at any depar-
ture which was not strictly according to the tastes of
cur seonini or imitation English. To these, as usual,
the mildly national section of the promoters deferred.
English and other foreign musicians were called over
to adjudicate ; and although there was a certain patron-
age of pipers and fiddlers, like the toleration of superior
| persons for a tumbler of whiskey punch after a fashion-
able cosmopolitan dinner of many and elaborate courses,
still the importance of the maintenance and study of
| our native folk music in its traditional purity as the

original matter which was to colour and distinguish
compositions of Irishmen from those of other nations,
was contemptuously ignored with results of sterilising
much of the good work the Feis Ceoil was accom-

plishing in the general cultivation of music.
The Feis Ceoil will have to be reformed, or else it
will die out before the national advance of the country.
N That it is only kept alive by the artificial support of
the seonini is seen in the sparse attendance at all gene-
ral meetings, which will grow ever sparser unless the
; Feis Ceoil is made in reality what it now pretends to
be—the national music festival. Who ever saw such
scanty meetings in organisations like the Gaelic T.eague
which is a vital movement ? The music movement to
be a success should be allied to the language move-
ment. The Feis Ceoil should by right be the musical
seetion of the Gaelic League, doing for Irish music
what the other does for the Irish language. Of course
this need not' mean that it should adopt the methods
g of the Gaelic League towards music. - These methods
: are not altogether satisfactory. As far as they aim
< % towards the preservation and encouragement of our foll
o music they are well. And it must be remembered to
its high credit that the Gaelic TLeague was the first to
produce an original opera by an Irishman in the Irish

language.., Still there is no, attempt by the Gaelic
League to promote the complete study and practise of
the art of Music, as the Feis Ceoil has promoted them,
notwithstanding its limitations. and mistakes. It is to
perfect the Feis Ceoil by example of Gaelic League
methods in the cultivation of Irish music and the adop;
tion of every -other thing Irish, while maintaining and
developing its already excellent work for the promotion
of music in general, that I advocate those reforms which
I think will result in a new and more vigorous life to
the movement. -

Above all, the importation of Inglish or other
foreign musicians as adjudicators should be done away
with. Their presence was always a mistake, even from
the first, notwithstanding the plea that we would,
thereby have more detached, and fairer judgments, and
that the Irish people would have more confidence in
foreigners’ decisions than in those of her own musicians.
The contrary has been the fact. The Irish people,
naturally irreverent, and rendered more so by recent
reading and teaching, only laugh at those lisping epi-
cenes and garrulous Cockneys, who come over here lay-
ing down the law, and showing off to us Irish. The
game of bringing in the foreigner seems nearly played
out.

If we geb rid of the English adjudicators it will be a
relief. Tancy, I heard one of those atrocities making
facetious remarks about the Irish language before an
audience of Irish people! Why should we employ them ?
We do not want them. Have we not several of our
own musicians to do the work, and at probably a.quar-
ter of the expense? In this way we could afford to
employ at least three judges in each competition who
would be a check upon each other, and would at least
avoid such a deplorable arithmetical mistake in marks
as recently caused so much dissatisfaction both to
competitors and the Feis executive.

I should like to Lknow, indeed, whether at
Inglish competitions they send to Ireland, or
any country other than Ingland, for adjudica-
tors.  Why, therefore, should we send to Eng-
land?  Already our countrymen have given us
satisfacfion in the persons of Mr. Joseph O’Mara,
My, Plunket Greene, and the late much-lamented
Denis O’Sullivan. We do not mind how severely we are
criticised by _our own people, but we .object
to be patronised by the English. Let this re-
form then be carried out, and let the acid and
brindled of the committee strive to get over their anti-
national prejudices, and to get more in touch with Irish
movements and [ife. I have always advocated that
the Teis Ceoil should be the musical branch of the
Gaelic League. It is a pity the promoters of the Feis
Ceoil will not see this. They are really the most
charming people in-the world—so chaming, indeed,
that it is always a pleasure to meet them or go to their
meetings, even if they will not do what you require.
Tor I always liked agreeable people—to my cost some
perhaps may think. N

If only those charming imitation English could be
persuaded to become real Irish, what a national success
the Feis Ceoil might be! The change will come at last
with Home Rule which, with its power and prestige,
will have at least this good effect of power and prestige.
Tt will always bring the flunkeys irto line.

AN FEACH DUBH.
THE BLACK RAVEN OF THE DANES.

NINE centuries ago Brian himself and four of his
sons, with his grandson, Turlach, yiclded up
' their lives for Ireland at the Battle of Clontarf. It
was on a Holy Friday, and so an added gloom was on
the Irish. Great preparations had been made for the
fight, long-studied calculations of the forces to be
engaged. Throughout the day the banner of the earl
Sigurd was seen to wave and fall, and wave again, and
yet again. It was a great banner, and had the earl’s
reverence and affection, for it had been made by his
own mother’s hands.  *‘ Take it, my son,’”’ she said,
““ take it with thee into all thy battles and it will ever
bring victory to thee.”” And so Sigurd believed, Sigurd,
earl of the Orkneys and the Shetlands, drawing the
tribute of Scotland from the Hebrides to the wall of
Adrian. Had he not fallen before the son of Brian at
Clontarf, he and Canute would have annexed all Eng-
land and changed the dynasty and the destiny of that
land for ever.

Earl Sigurd, if victorious, was to have wed Gorm-
laith, the sister of his slayer, Murchad, the daughter
of king Brian. He would also have been acclaimed
Ardrigh.

Sigurd’s banner, fresh and fair from his mother’s
hands, waved proudly, almost victoriously, amongst
the fierce Norsemen and the Fingall, or fair foreigners,
on that battle field along the strand of Dublin’s Bay
and up the Tolka. It was a proud and haughty banner,
ever borne into the press of battle, ever held full blown
by the breeze from the sca, borne aloft with the strength
of sinewy arms and strident thighs, ever where the fight
was sorest there was Sigurd’s banner—the banner of
the Feach Dubh, the black raven—‘‘ And when,” as
the old saga puts it, * the wind blew on the banner then
it was as though the raven flapped its wings as if for
flight.””

%ne bearer after another fell under that banner until
“three had fallen, and it seemed as if the earl’s mother’s
words were to have mo meaning. Sigurd snatched it
from the dying grasp of the last to fall, and bore it away
until he too fell, smitten to the heart by the mighty
sword of Murchad, son of Brian, Then the invaders

fled, as queen Gormlaith said, ‘“See the Norsemen
how they fly, they run

The sun went down on Brian slain in his tent, and
the reeking bodies of his four sons and young Turlach,
his grandson, cast back by the tide, still clenching their
hacked and broken swords in their death grasp,
entwined with the hair of the invader and covered by
the bronzed weeds of the sea.

Brian had willed it that Armagh was to be his burial®
place. Years before he had tarried around the seat of
Saint Patrick near royal Emania, and had placed upon
the high altar a heavy collar of gold and had marked
out his burial place. ~ With his sons, Murchad and
Turlagh, he was waked in Saint Columba’s Monastery,
at Swords, the first night of his burial march. Then
for three other days, through Lusk and Meath, and
Louth and Armagh, the aged bones of the greatest of
the Ardrigh were borne, and at last laid at the south
side of the high altar of Saint Patrick’s famous shrine.

That was the greatest funeral pageant that ever
crossed Ireland. In fancy we can see it still. The
kingly head on the bier borne shoulder high by strong
kilted warriors, the sacred cross in the dead king’s
hand, his sword by his side, his sons’ bodies borne
after him—the long lines of white-robed clerics chant-
ing the Latin psalms as they go, or raising the Gaelic
hymn or wail for the dead. Columns of warriors trailed
their spears with bent heads, pipers wailed the caoine
and the drums sobbed as Brian was borne to his burial.
The captured standard of earl Sigurd was carried low, -
as a trophy hardly won in that long-drawn contest.
Fit emblem of conquered pride at the funeral of a dead
monarch and so one and all, the clergy at their office,
the pipers at their requiem, and the black raven stan:
dard captured for Ireland bespoke deeply the feelings
of a mourning nation. .

The black raven standard of earl Sigurd and his
Fingalls fell before the son of Brian of the tributes,
and he held it as a trophy for Ireland. To-day the
Feach Dubh is seen again floating over the green mea- -
dows of the Lusk of Fingal, under the branches of tall

trees, and along the winding roads past the high towers

of the old abbey, with the

piob mor behind 1t playing

the march of Brian Boru and all for Ireland.

The fair foreigners have

mingled with the older race

and all are now one in work and emulation, striving to

make the old language of

the Gael heard once again

WE steamed into the Glanmire- terminus punc-
' ' tually to time, and were greeted with the
inspiring. strains of ‘A Nation Once Again ” from one
of the Rebel City’s famous bands: A cheer went up
as. the well-known strain fell on our ears; and though
- primarily bent on pleasure, we had someliow an intan-
gll}le idea at the back of our heads that we were some-
thing. moré than mere excursionists—we had striven
to keep our country true to its old proud traditions and
had’ failed—for the time being! Still we felt neither
ashamed nor dismayed. We had the conscious pride
of belonging to ‘‘the one true fold,” at one with our
scattered race the world over; and the mckname our
poor misguided countrymen had bestowed on us as a
term of reproach we had converted into a term
of glory. ~We were ‘‘Mollies,” proud of our
cause and unashamed of the origin of our name. We
felt, moreover, that we possessed that infallible hall-
mark of true nationality: we had the hatred of the
hereditary foes of our race. No Carson or Craig ever
besmirched our spotless banner with his praises. When
men taunted us unjustly with the action of our coun-
trymen in Wexford in '98, we could truthfully reply :
They were no countrymen of ours, they were the re-
tinue of the landlords imported from the North and
elsewhere. As logically might you blame the heroic
Nationalists of the West Division of Belfast for the
conduct of the Orangemen of the Shankill Road as the
men of Cork for the inhuman conduet of Cork Orange-
men in ‘98. But in the coming time when the stan-
dards' of battle are furled and our children ask us of
these heroic days, what shall we say? This at least:
A section of our countrymen, it is true, in an unholy
allianice with the enemy, left us in a minority, but all
that was good and true in the county were a tower of
strength' to the cause in its final stages.

Led by the band playing stirring national airs we
marched through the streets of the Rebel City to where
our boat, gaily decked with bunting, lay awaiting.

feast our eyes on river scenmery that is almost
matchless in Europe. Past the wooded, villa-crowned

is enshrined in Trish hearts for ever by her sad love
story and her connection with the noblest patriot in
our history. The domes and minarets of Constan-
tinople scarce look down on scenes more fair, every
rood of which is linked up with some memory of the
past. ITaving a deep reverence, even in these con-
stitutional days, for the men who had to adopt more
‘heroic methods, we all looked eagerly for a view of the
Martello tower at Fota which was captured by Captain

can be furthered in the Ivish-speaking districts. There
are various ways in.which something could be done if
we only set our minds to work, and each way when
tried would most likely open out possibilities not yet
foreseen. I will endeavour in this article to show
what could be done with advantage each summer while
the Irish Colleges are open, commencing with Ghis
year of 1912,
_ I think it is admitted that the Irish Colleges estab-
lished in the TIrish-speaking districts have been the
most successful mave yet made to fix the attention of
the native speakers as to the importance the language
holds with people living in other parts of Ireland.
They cannot but be impressed when they see people
who come from a distance at their own expense
and at a good deal of inconvenience to learn Irish, in
what, in many cases, is an out-of-the-way part of Ire-
land. Country people get to know particularly those
students who come to their district from England, and,
as has occasionally -happened, from America, The
more striking manner in which the Gaelic League pro-
paganda could be brought before their minds in some
practical way, in combination with the Irish College
sessions would, I think, be a very wise step.  And
now for my suggestion :—

I would suggest that the Coisde Gnotha of the Gaelic
League should, while: the Itish College sessions are
on, each year hold a Feis or some such large gathering
in two different Irish-speaking districts, to last a whole
week. I suppose there are only about six or eight real
good centres where Irish Colleges are established, and
so each centre would have its turn every -three or four
years. Such a gathering would, if properly carried
out, rouse the whole country-side, gnd set the inhabi-
tants speaking about it for the twelve months follow-
ing. Matters could be so arranged that nothing but
Irish would be spoken in the district by the visitors
for the whole week; at least when on their walks
abroad among the people. The Feis would draw out
the talent to be found in the country-side around and
the efforts should be largely directed to that end. If
it was known in the district that the fixture was orga-
nised from Dublin and that people in large numbers
came from a distance to attend it would have a great
effect. Then the presence of the Irish College stu-
dents, who would combine no doubt to make the event
a great success, would-bé a great incentive to the local
people to do their best. I respectfully submit that
this idea could be carried out with most advantageous
results in the Irish-speaking districts, and could be
tried in at least one district next month—August.
